addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Fast read-only search agent for locating code. Use it to find files by pattern (eg. "src/components/**/*.tsx"), grep for symbols or keywords (eg. "API endpoints"), or answer "where is X defined / which files reference Y." Do NOT use it for code review, design-doc auditing, cross-file consistency checks, or open-ended analysis — it reads excerpts rather than whole files and will miss content past its read window. When calling, specify search breadth: "quick" for a single targeted lookup, "medium" for moderate exploration, or "very thorough" to search across multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.

Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). services:
  agentsview:
    image: ghcr.io/kenn-io/agentsview:latest
    restart: unless-stopped
    ports:
      - "0.0.0.0:${AGENTSVIEW_PORT:-8081}:8080"

      - "--host"
      - "0.0.0.0"
      - "--no-browser"
      - "--public-url"
      - "http://${AGENTSVIEW_HOST:-localhost}:${AGENTSVIEW_PORT:-8081}"
    volumes:
      - agentsview-fullsend-data:/data
      - ${AGENTSVIEW_RUNS:-./runs}:/agents/claude:ro

volumes:
  agentsview-fullsend-data:

Syncs session data from supported AI coding agents into SQLite,

  pg                     PostgreSQL sync and serve commands
  pg push                Push local data to PostgreSQL
  pg serve               Serve from PostgreSQL (read-only)
  pg service             Install and manage the pg push --watch background service
  pg status              Show PG sync status
  secrets                Scan for and list detected secret leaks

  session messages       Show a window of messages from a session

  session sync           Parse and insert a single session into the database

Sync session data into the local database without starting the
HTTP server.

With no --host, sync runs the local sync and then fans out to
every host listed in the [[remote_hosts]] array in config.toml,
syncing each over SSH. A failure on one configured host is logged
and the run continues; the command exits non-zero if any
configured host failed.

With --host, sync ignores remote_hosts and syncs only that host.

Remote sync uses your existing SSH configuration and requires
key-based (passwordless) auth; it never prompts for a password.

## AgentsView by kenn-io — Machine Filter & Multi-Machine Configuration

### Overview

AgentsView provides local-first session search, analytics, insights, and token use statistics for coding agents, supporting Claude Code, Codex, and more than 20 other agents.

### CLAUDE_PROJECTS_DIR Configuration

When running via Docker, you can mount Claude session directories and set the `CLAUDE_PROJECTS_DIR` environment variable to point AgentsView at your Claude sessions. The Docker example maps `$HOME/.claude/projects` to `/agents/claude` inside the container and sets `CLAUDE_PROJECTS_DIR=/agents/claude`.

The full list of supported environment variable overrides includes many agents. For example:
`CLAUDE_PROJECTS_DIR`, `OPENCLAUDE_PROJECTS_DIR`, `CODEX_SESSIONS_DIR`, `FORGE_DIR`, `CURSOR_PROJECTS_DIR`, `GEMINI_DIR`, and many more can be set to customize session discovery paths.

### Multi-Machine Configuration via S3

Claude and Codex sources can also be configured as `s3://` roots, so a central AgentsView instance can read sessions that other machines push to S3-compatible object storage.

The S3 path convention follows a structure that encodes the machine name:
The path format is `s3://bucket/.../<machine>/raw/claude/<project>/<uuid>.jsonl`, where the machine name is derived from the path segment immediately before `raw`. If no such segment exists, sessions use the local AgentsView machine label.

### Machine Filter & Session Filtering

Sessions can be filtered by project, agent, date, or message count. Sessions can also be filtered by status from the filter dropdown or via the new `?termination=` URL parameter. Recent releases added branch metadata/filter support for session queries.

### S3 Sync Efficiency

S3 object Size, LastModified, and available object fingerprints (ETag, version ID, and checksum headers) are stored in the session row and used for unchanged-object skip checks. A later sync therefore lists object metadata first and downloads only objects whose source metadata changed or whose stored parser data is stale.

### Usage & Cost Tracking

AgentsView tracks token consumption and compute costs across all your coding agents — not just Claude Code. Because session data is already indexed in SQLite, queries are over 100x faster than tools that re-parse raw session files on every run.

### Recent Updates (2026)

- v0.31.1 (2026-05-28) added fallback pricing for Claude Opus 4.8 so cost estimates stay accurate when live pricing data is unavailable.
- v0.32.0 (2026-06-04) added support for Antigravity CLI SQLite sessions.
- A recent release also fixed Visual Studio 2026 Copilot session parsing.

### Deployment Options

For a PostgreSQL-backed deployment, you can point the container at your shared database using `PG_SERVE=1` and `AGENTSVIEW_PG_URL`. There's also a DuckDB-based remote deployment option via the Quack protocol for multi-machine setups.

In /Users/mhild/src/rhdh/fullsend-sessions/agentsview/, find how the "machine" field is determined for sessions during local sync. I'm specifically looking for:

1. Where the string "local" is set as the default machine name for locally-synced sessions
2. Whether there's any config option or path-based logic that determines the machine name
3. The code path from sync → session parsing → database insert that sets the machine field

Search for "machine" in the Go source code, especially in files related to sync, session parsing, and database operations. Report back the relevant file paths, line numbers, and the key logic.

content.text:
I have the full picture now. Here is the complete analysis:

---

## How the "machine" field is determined for sessions during local sync

### 1. Where "local" is set as the default machine name

The string `"local"` is hardcoded in **every** `sync.NewEngine` call site in `cmd/agentsview/`. There is no config option for this value -- it is always the literal string `"local"`.

All call sites:

| File | Line | Context |
|------|------|---------|
| `cmd/agentsview/sync.go` | 468 | `runLocalSync` (CLI `sync` command) |
| `cmd/agentsview/main.go` | 208 | daemon/serve startup |
| `cmd/agentsview/session_sync.go` | 75 | single-session sync |
| `cmd/agentsview/archive_write_backend.go` | 550 | archive write |
| `cmd/agentsview/archive_query_backend.go` | 250 | archive query |
| `cmd/agentsview/parse_diff.go` | 128 | parse-diff (NewDiffEngine) |
| `cmd/agentsview/usage.go` | 311, 335 | usage commands |

Every one of them passes `Machine: "local"` in the `sync.EngineConfig`.

### 2. No config option for the local machine name

There is **no** config option to override the local sync machine name. The `machine_name` config keys in `config.go` exist only for **PG** and **DuckDB** remote push sync targets:

- `/agentsview/internal/config/config.go:64` -- `PGConfig.MachineName` (TOML key `machine_name`)
- `/agentsview/internal/config/config.go:98` -- `DuckDBConfig.MachineName` (TOML key `machine_name`)

These default to `os.Hostname()` when unset (lines 2089-2094 and 2204-2209) and are used only for push sync to PostgreSQL/DuckDB, **not** for local SQLite sync.

The PostgreSQL sync (`internal/postgres/sync.go:217-222`) explicitly **rejects** `"local"` as a machine name:

```go
if machine == "local" {
    return nil, fmt.Errorf(
        "machine name %q is reserved; "+
            "choose a different pg.machine_name",
        machine,
    )
}
```

This is documented on line 200: `"local"` is reserved as "the SQLite sentinel for sessions that originated on this machine."

### 3. Code path: sync -> session parsing -> database insert

The full chain:

1. **Engine construction**: `sync.NewEngine(db, sync.EngineConfig{Machine: "local"})` stores `"local"` in `engine.machine` (`internal/sync/engine.go:258`).

2. **Provider creation**: When syncing, the engine creates providers with `parser.ProviderConfig{Machine: e.machine}` (e.g., lines 716, 2254, 2558, 3121, 3173, 3881).

3. **Parse request**: The `SourceSetProvider.Parse` method (in `internal/parser/source_set.go:93`) populates the `ParseRequest.Machine` using `firstNonEmptyJSONLString(req.Machine, p.Config.Machine)`. Since `req.Machine` is typically empty for local sessions, it falls back to `p.Config.Machine` which is `"local"`.

4. **Parser sets Machine on ParsedSession**: Each agent parser (e.g., `internal/parser/claude.go:832`) sets `Machine: machine` on the `ParsedSession` struct.

5. **Conversion to db.Session**: `toDBSession()` (`internal/sync/engine.go:7779`) copies `Machine: pw.sess.Machine` (the `ParsedSession.Machine` value, which is `"local"`).

6. **Database insert**: `db.UpsertSession(s)` is called (`engine.go:7430`), which runs an INSERT/UPDATE via `upsertSessionArgs()` (`internal/db/sessions.go:1296`) that includes `s.Machine` as the third column.

**Exception for S3/remote sources**: When session files come from S3 (`engine.go:2326`), the `DiscoveredFile.Machine` is set from the S3 source metadata (`s3.Machine`), which is an actual machine identifier rather than `"local"`. This is how remote machines get their real hostname in the machine field.

content.content:
# AGENTS.md

Instructions for autonomous coding agents working in this repository.

## Scope

- Applies to all agent-driven work in this repo.
- If multiple instruction files exist, follow the most specific one for the
  files you are editing.

## Required Git Rules

1. Commit every turn that changes tracked files.
1. Do not make empty commits. If a turn is read-only or only changes ignored
   files, state that no commit was made.
1. Do not amend commits.
1. Do not change branches without explicit user permission.
1. Do not merge pull requests. Open them and report status; merging is
   always the user's decision, even when checks are green and the change
   is urgent.

## Commit Expectations

- Keep commits focused and related to the requested task.
- Use clear conventional commit messages.
- Do not push, pull, or rebase unless explicitly requested.
- Do not include generated-with lines, attribution blocks, validation footers,
  or command transcripts in commit messages.

## Validation

- Run relevant tests before committing when practical.
- If tests cannot be run, state that clearly in the handoff.
- After Go code changes, run `go fmt ./...` and `go vet ./...` before
  committing.

## Backend Parity

- Preserve behavior and query-shape parity between supported storage backends
  whenever practical. SQLite and PostgreSQL/Cockroach queries, indexes,
  aggregations, filtering, and ordering should match until there is a
  concrete, documented reason for them to differ.
- Do not implement a performance or correctness fix for only one backend and
  call the problem solved unless the user explicitly scopes the work to that
  backend, for example "this is only for PostgreSQL". If one backend needs a
  different implementation, explain why and keep the observable behavior the
  same.

## Localization

- Keep frontend message catalogs synchronized. When adding, removing, or
  renaming user-facing message keys in `frontend/messages/*.json`, update
  every locale listed in `frontend/project.inlang/settings.json` in the same
  turn and keep the key sets identical across locales.
- After message catalog or localized component changes, run
  `npm run i18n:compile` and `npm run check` from `frontend/` when practical.

## Test Style

- Go tests use `github.com/stretchr/testify` for assertions. Use `require.X`
  when a failed check should abort the test (setup, nil receivers, length
  checks before indexing) and `assert.X` for independent checks that should
  keep running. Don't write `if got != want { t.Fatalf(...) }` in new tests.
- Domain-specific helpers are fine, but they must use testify internally rather
  than stdlib comparisons.

## Safety

- Do not revert user-authored or unrelated local changes unless explicitly
  requested.
- Avoid destructive git commands unless explicitly requested.
- The SQLite database is a persistent archive. Never delete, drop, truncate, or
  recreate it to handle data version changes. Schema changes use
  non-destructive migrations such as `ALTER TABLE` and `UPDATE`; parser
  changes trigger a full resync that builds a fresh DB, syncs files, copies
  orphaned sessions from the old DB, and swaps atomically. Existing session
  data must be preserved even when source files no longer exist on disk.

## Project Overview

agentsview is a local web viewer for AI agent sessions. It syncs session data
from disk into SQLite with FTS5 full-text search, serves a Svelte 5 SPA via an
embedded Go HTTP server, and provides real-time updates via SSE. See
`internal/parser/types.go` for the full list of supported agents.

## Architecture

```text
CLI (agentsview) -> Config -> DB (SQLite/FTS5)
                  |           |
                  v           v
              File Watcher -> Sync Engine -> Parsers (per agent)
                  |           |
                  v           v
              HTTP Server -> REST API + SSE + Embedded SPA
                              |
                              v
                           PG Push Sync -> PostgreSQL (optional)
                              ^
                              |
              HTTP Server (pg serve) <- PostgreSQL
```

- Server: HTTP server with auto-port discovery, defaulting to 8080.
- Storage: SQLite with WAL mode, FTS5 for full-text search, and optional
  PostgreSQL for multi-machine shared access.
- Sync: file watcher plus periodic sync every 15 minutes for session
  directories.
- PG sync: on-demand push sync from SQLite to PostgreSQL via `pg push`.
- Frontend: Svelte 5 SPA embedded in the Go binary at build time.
- Config: `AGENTSVIEW_DATA_DIR` plus per-agent directory overrides and CLI
  flags. Per-agent env vars are listed on each entry in
  `internal/parser/types.go`.

## Project Structure

- `cmd/agentsview/` - Go server entrypoint.
- `cmd/testfixture/` - Test data generator for E2E tests.
- `internal/config/` - Config loading, JSON migration, and flag registration.
- `internal/db/` - SQLite sessions, messages, search, analytics, and schema.
- `internal/postgres/` - PostgreSQL push sync, read-only store, schema, and
  connection helpers.
- `internal/parser/` - Per-agent session file parsers and content extraction.
- `internal/server/` - HTTP handlers, SSE, middleware, search, and export.
- `internal/sync/` - Sync engine, file watcher, discovery, and hashing.
- `internal/vector/` - Semantic search: embeddings encoder, `vectors.db`
  mirror/index, build orchestration, and semantic/hybrid search.
- `internal/timeutil/` - Time parsing utilities.
- `internal/web/` - Embedded frontend copied from `frontend/dist/` at build
  time.
- `frontend/` - Svelte 5 SPA with Vite and TypeScript.
- `scripts/` - Utility scripts for E2E server setup and changelog work.

## Key Files

| Path                             | Purpose                                                   |
| -------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- |
| `cmd/agentsview/main.go`         | CLI entry point, server startup, file watcher             |
| `cmd/agentsview/pg.go`           | `pg` command group: push, status, serve                   |
| `cmd/agentsview/embeddings.go`   | `embeddings` command group: build, list, activate, retire |
| `internal/server/server.go`      | HTTP router and handler setup                             |
| `internal/server/sessions.go`    | Session list/detail API handlers                          |
| `internal/server/search.go`      | Full-text search API                                      |
| `internal/server/events.go`      | SSE event streaming                                       |
| `internal/db/db.go`              | Database open, migrations, schema                         |
| `internal/db/sessions.go`        | Session CRUD queries                                      |

## Development

```bash
make build          # Build binary with embedded frontend
make dev            # Run Go server in dev mode
make frontend       # Build frontend SPA only
make frontend-dev   # Run Vite dev server, use alongside make dev
make install        # Build and install to ~/.local/bin or GOPATH
make install-hooks  # Install pre-commit and pre-push git hooks
```

## Testing

All new features and bug fixes must include unit tests. Run tests before
committing:

```bash
make test       # Go tests with CGO_ENABLED=1 and -tags "fts5"
make test-short # Fast tests only with -short
make e2e        # Playwright E2E tests
make lint       # golangci-lint plus NilAway
make vet        # go vet
```

## Test Style

- Prefer table-driven tests for Go code.
- Go tests use `github.com/stretchr/testify` for assertions.
- Use `require.X` when a failed check should abort the test, including setup
  errors, nil receivers, and length checks before indexing.
- Use `assert.X` for independent checks that should keep running.
- Do not write `if got != want { t.Fatalf(...) }` in new tests.
- Domain-specific helpers are fine, but they must use testify internally rather
  than stdlib comparisons.
- Use the existing `testDB(t)` helper for database tests.
- Frontend tests are colocated `*.test.ts` files, with Playwright specs in
  `frontend/e2e/`.
- All tests use `t.TempDir()` for temp directories.
- Shell script tests must exercise observable behavior by running the script
  against controlled inputs and asserting outputs, side effects, or exit
  codes. Do not write tautological tests that read a shell script and assert
  that it contains a specific implementation line, flag, or snippet.

## PostgreSQL Integration Tests

PG integration tests require a real PostgreSQL instance and the `pgtest` build
tag. The easiest way to run them is with docker-compose:

```bash
make test-postgres   # Starts PG container, runs tests, leaves container running
make postgres-down   # Stop the test container when done
```

Or manually with an existing PostgreSQL instance:

```bash
TEST_PG_URL="postgres://user:pass@host:5432/dbname?sslmode=disable" \
  CGO_ENABLED=1 go test -tags "fts5,pgtest" ./internal/postgres/... -v
```

Tests create and drop the `agentsview` schema, so use a dedicated database or
one where schema changes are acceptable. The CI pipeline runs these tests via a
GitHub Actions service container in `.github/workflows/ci.yml`.

## Build Requirements

- `CGO_ENABLED=1` is required for the sqlite3 driver.
- The `fts5` build tag is required for full-text search.
- `go test` does not need kit's `kit_posthog_disabled` build tag. The telemetry
  reporter already short-circuits to a disabled no-op under
  `testing.Testing()`, so tests never send PostHog events. Binaries built for
  e2e tests (`make e2e`, the CI pre-build of `agentsview`/`testfixture`) do
  use the tag, because they run as real processes where the test guard does
  not apply.
- Node.js and npm are required to build the Svelte frontend embedded under
  `internal/web/dist/`.
- The frontend depends on `@kenn-io/kit-ui` as a git dependency pinned to a
  commit (`git+https://github.com/kenn-io/kit-ui.git#<commit>` in
  `frontend/package.json`). The repository is public, so `npm ci`/
  `npm install` clone it anonymously over HTTPS with no credentials; the only
  requirement is git on PATH. The lockfile records the dependency as
  `git+ssh://git@github.com/...` — that is npm's canonical form for
  GitHub-hosted git deps and cannot be changed, but npm still fetches over
  anonymous HTTPS (verified with SSH disabled and a cold cache); do not "fix"
  it. Bump the dependency by changing the commit hash in `frontend/package.json`
  and running `npm install`.

## Conventions

- Prefer stdlib over external dependencies.
- Tests should be fast and isolated.
- No emojis in code or output.
- For frontend UI work, read `DESIGN.md` before adding or changing controls,
  styling, or reusable components.
- Use `mdformat --wrap 80` to format Markdown files when mdformat and
  `mdformat-tables` are available.

## Pull Requests

- PR descriptions should be summaries only, with no test plans or checklists. Do
  not add a "Tests", "Testing", "Verification", or "Test plan" section. CI
  runs the tests, so the description must not restate the suite, list test
  commands, or describe how the change was verified.
- Describe what the code does now, why it changed, tradeoffs, limitations, and
  where reviewers should look.

content.content:
agentsview dev - local web viewer for AI agent sessions

Syncs session data from supported AI coding agents into SQLite,
serves analytics, and exposes a session browser via local web UI.

Usage:
  agentsview [flags]
  agentsview <command> [flags]

Core Commands:
  health                 Show session health and signals
  projects               List projects with session counts
  prune                  Delete sessions matching filters
  serve                  Start server
  sync                   Sync session data without serving

Data Commands:
  duckdb                 DuckDB sync and serve commands
  duckdb push            Push local data to DuckDB
  duckdb quack           Quack remote protocol commands
  duckdb serve           Serve from DuckDB (read-only)
  duckdb status          Show DuckDB sync status
  import                 Import conversations
  parse-diff             Re-parse session files and diff against the archive
  pg                     PostgreSQL sync and serve commands
  pg push                Push local data to PostgreSQL
  pg serve               Serve from PostgreSQL (read-only)
  pg service             Install and manage the pg push --watch background service
  pg status              Show PG sync status
  secrets                Scan for and list detected secret leaks
  secrets list           List detected secret findings (redacted by default)
  secrets scan           Run a full-ruleset scan (definite + candidate rules) and persist findings
  session                Programmatic access to session data
  session export         Stream the raw source JSONL for a session (local only)
  session get            Get session metadata and signals
  session list           List sessions with filters
  session messages       Show a window of messages from a session
  session search         Search message and tool content across sessions
  session sync           Parse and insert a single session into the database
  session tool-calls     List tool calls made during a session
  session usage          Show token usage and cost estimate for a session
  session watch          Stream NDJSON events as the session updates
  stats                  Window-scoped workspace analytics (v1 schema)
  token-use              Show token usage for a session (JSON)

Usage Commands:
  usage                  Token cost tracking and reporting
  usage daily            Daily cost summary
  usage statusline       One-line cost summary for today

Other Commands:
  completion             Generate the autocompletion script for the specified shell
  openapi                Print OpenAPI 3.1 schema
  update                 Check for and install updates
  version                Show version information


Flags:
  -h, --help      help for agentsview
  -v, --version   Show version information
Environment variables:
  CLAUDE_PROJECTS_DIR     Claude Code projects directory
  CODEX_SESSIONS_DIR      Codex sessions directory
  COPILOT_DIR             Copilot CLI directory
  GEMINI_DIR              Gemini CLI directory
  OPENCODE_DIR            OpenCode data directory
  CURSOR_PROJECTS_DIR     Cursor projects directory
  IFLOW_DIR               iFlow projects directory
  AMP_DIR                 Amp threads directory
  ZED_DIR                 Zed data directory
  QWEN_PROJECTS_DIR       Qwen Code projects directory
  QCLAW_DIR               QClaw agents directory
  WORKBUDDY_PROJECTS_DIR  WorkBuddy projects directory
  PIEBALD_DIR             Piebald data directory
  AGENTSVIEW_DATA_DIR     Data directory (database, config)
  AGENTSVIEW_PG_URL       PostgreSQL connection URL for sync
  AGENTSVIEW_PG_MACHINE   Machine name for PG sync
  AGENTSVIEW_PG_SCHEMA    PG schema name (default "agentsview")
  AGENTSVIEW_DUCKDB_PATH  DuckDB mirror database path
  AGENTSVIEW_DUCKDB_URL   Quack connection URL for DuckDB serve
  AGENTSVIEW_DUCKDB_TOKEN Quack authentication token
  AGENTSVIEW_DUCKDB_MACHINE
                          Machine name for DuckDB sync

Watcher excludes:
  Add "watch_exclude_patterns" to ~/.agentsview/config.toml
  to skip directory names/patterns while recursively watching roots.
  Example:
  watch_exclude_patterns = [".git", "node_modules", ".next", "dist"]

Multiple directories:
  Add arrays to ~/.agentsview/config.toml to scan multiple locations:
  claude_project_dirs = ["/path/one", "/path/two"]
  codex_sessions_dirs = ["/codex/a", "/codex/b"]
  When set, these override default directory. Environment variables
  override config file arrays.

Remote hosts:
  Add a [[remote_hosts]] array to ~/.agentsview/config.toml so that
  "agentsview sync" (no --host) also syncs each host over SSH:
  [[remote_hosts]]
  host = "devbox1"
  user = "jesse"  # optional
  port = 22        # optional
  Each host must be unique.
  Requires key-based (passwordless) SSH to each host.

Data stored in ~/.agentsview/ by default.

**For local sync, the machine is always `"local"` — hardcoded, no config option.** The machine concept was built for multi-machine aggregation:

content.text:
Interesting issue — #332 was about PG push overwriting the original machine name. It's closed (presumably fixed), which means PG push now preserves per-session machine provenance.

Now to your questions:

**Can Claude write sessions directly to S3?**

No. Claude Code writes session transcripts to `~/.claude/projects/<hash>/` on the local filesystem — there's no S3 configuration. Your export hook already copies sessions to a shared repo. You *could* extend it to upload to S3, but Claude itself doesn't support it natively.

**Can we reuse the existing multi-machine mechanisms?**

Let me evaluate each:

| Mechanism | How machine is set | Compose feasibility |
|---|---|---|
| **SSH remote hosts** | From the SSH hostname | Would need per-user SSH containers — absurd overhead |
| **S3 sources** | Parsed from path: `s3://…/<machine>/raw/claude/…` | Would need MinIO + restructured uploads — doable but heavy |
| **PG push** | `pg.machine_name` config | One machine name per push — can't differentiate users in one pass |

All three were designed for genuinely separate machines. Shoehorning them for "multiple users in one directory" adds significant complexity for something that should be a one-line config.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The S3 path convention (`s3://bucket/prefix/<machine>/raw/claude/<project>/<uuid>.jsonl`) is the closest match — it already parses the machine from the path. A MinIO sidecar in compose could work, but you'd need to restructure the export hook to upload sessions into per-user S3 paths instead of flat directories. That's a lot of moving parts for what amounts to a missing config field.

Issue #332 confirms that the machine provenance chain is well-understood in the codebase — local sync is simply the one path that never got a config option because it was designed as single-user-per-machine.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**My recommendation**: The existing mechanisms are all designed for real multi-machine setups. Wrapping any of them in compose for your use case adds complexity without benefit. The right fix is a small AgentsView code change — either:

1. A top-level `machine_name` config (trivial, mirrors PG/DuckDB pattern)
2. Or better: a `machine_from_cwd_regex` that extracts the machine from the session's `cwd` path — which would naturally solve your `<user>_<project>` convention

You have the source. Want me to draft the code change? It would touch ~3 files: `config.go`, `sync.go`, and `main.go`.

gh issue create --repo durandom/fullsend-sessions \
  --title "Switch session storage from git to S3 for per-user machine names in AgentsView" \
  --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Problem

AgentsView hardcodes `machine: "local"` for all locally-synced sessions. Our shared session directory (`sessions/<user>_<project>/<uuid>.jsonl`) shows every session under a single "local" machine, making it impossible to filter by user in the AgentsView UI.

## Proposed Solution

Switch from git-based session sharing to S3-compatible object storage (e.g. MinIO in podman compose, or a shared S3 bucket).

AgentsView already supports S3 sources and **automatically parses the machine name from the S3 path convention**:

```
s3://bucket/<machine>/raw/claude/<project>/<uuid>.jsonl
```

By uploading sessions as:

```
s3://fullsend-sessions/marcel-hild/raw/claude/fullsend-sessions/<uuid>.jsonl
s3://fullsend-sessions/christoph-jerolimov/raw/claude/rhdh-plugins/<uuid>.jsonl
```

AgentsView will treat `marcel-hild` and `christoph-jerolimov` as distinct machines — giving us per-user filtering for free with zero AgentsView code changes.

## What needs to change

1. **Compose setup** — add a MinIO (or compatible) service to `compose.yaml`, or configure a shared S3 bucket
2. **Export script** (`scripts/export-session.sh`) — replace git commit/push with S3 upload (`aws s3 cp` or `mc cp`), using the path convention `<username>/raw/claude/<project>/<session-id>.jsonl`
3. **AgentsView config** — configure `claude_project_dirs` with the `s3://` root in `config.toml`
4. **Sessions env** (`~/.config/fullsend/sessions.env`) — add S3 credentials/endpoint config

## Benefits

- Per-user machine names in AgentsView (the original goal)
- No more git merge conflicts or rebase issues on concurrent session pushes
- Simpler export hook (S3 PUT vs git add/commit/pull --rebase/push)
- AgentsView gets real-time file watching via S3 polling (already built in)

## Current setup for reference

```
SessionEnd hook → export-session.sh → git add → git commit → git pull --rebase → git push
AgentsView reads from local sessions/ directory → all sessions get machine="local"
```

## Open questions

- Self-hosted MinIO in compose vs. shared cloud S3 bucket?
- Keep git repo as archive/backup alongside S3, or fully migrate?
- Credential distribution: per-user S3 keys vs. shared write token?
EOF
)"

Issue created: https://github.com/durandom/fullsend-sessions/issues/1

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The S3 approach elegantly solves two problems at once:

1. **Per-user machine names** — AgentsView's S3 discovery already extracts the machine from `s3://bucket/<machine>/raw/claude/…`, so structuring uploads as `<username>/raw/claude/<project>/<uuid>.jsonl` gives you user-based filtering with zero AgentsView changes.

2. **Concurrent write conflicts** — The current git-based flow (`commit → pull --rebase → push`) is inherently racy when multiple users export simultaneously. S3 PUTs are idempotent and conflict-free — each user writes to their own key prefix, so there's no coordination needed.

A MinIO container in the existing podman compose setup would keep everything self-hosted and local, while a shared cloud bucket would work for distributed teams. The export hook change is minimal: replace `git add/commit/push` with a single `mc cp` or `aws s3 cp`.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`
